text:
Structure-Aware and Intervention-Sensitive Traffic Transformers with Soft Physical Regularization and Dynamic Consistency Hans Matthew E. Del Mundo, Gerald P. Helbiro Jr., Ira Chloie C. Narisma, Vin Marcus Nicolo Gerebise 1 Background of the Study Urban mobility systems are increasingly instrumented by loop detectors, connected sensing platforms, and digital traffic infrastructure, enabling data-driven forecasting and control at city scale [5, 6, 16]. This data availability has accelerated the shift from classical statistical formulations toward deep spatio-temporal architectures that represent both temporal regularities and non-Euclidean road-network structure [2, 19, 23]. The practical consequence is a strong increase in forecasting accuracy under recurring traffic regimes, where historical patterns are informative and model assumptions are not heavily stressed.
However, the way transformer architectures are commonly operationalized in traffic forecasting remains narrower than their contextual potential. In language modeling, transformer performance is driven by rich contextual encoding of syntax, semantics, and long-range dependencies; by contrast, many traffic implementations still treat observations primarily as autoregressive temporal tokens and use graph structure as a static bias or auxiliary feature rather than a central contextual object of reasoning [3, 14, 18, 23]. This creates a representational gap: the model can fit trajectories, yet still fail to internalize why congestion propagates differently across links with distinct structural roles.
At the same time, planning and policy use cases demand models that remain stable when the operating regime changes due to lane closures, capacity reductions, disruptions, or demand surges. In these settings, short-horizon correlation fitting is often insufficient because network responses are governed by constrained dynamics and spillover effects that can propagate far from the point of intervention [10, 22, 25]. This gap between benchmark prediction and intervention reliability motivates the present concept paper.
The limitations observed in the broader survey context are consistent with this concern: weak transferability across cities, limited robustness under distribution shift, and insufficiently integrated interpretability for intervention reasoning [7, 12, 21, 25]. Taken together, these gaps suggest that better structural understanding is not only a modeling preference but a prerequisite for building generalizable traffic intelligence.
The existing methodological landscape is summarized in Table 1. The table is included to clarify how the proposed concept is positioned relative to prior paradigms and why a structure-aware, intervention-sensitive design is needed despite recent advances in graph transformers and hybrid attention models.
2 Overview of Existing Approaches Recent traffic forecasting literature can be grouped into three streams that directly inform this concept.
First, STGNN an

s and why a structure-aware, intervention-sensitive design is needed despite recent advances in graph transformers and hybrid attention models.
2 Overview of Existing Approaches Recent traffic forecasting literature can be grouped into three streams that directly inform this concept.
First, STGNN and dynamic-graph models consistently show strong local forecasting performance by exploiting neighborhood message passing, but they can still face sensitivity to graph construction choices and bottlenecks in long-range propagation modeling [1, 7, 11, 19]. Second, transformer and hybrid-attention approaches improve global receptive fields and cross-scale pattern capture, yet many implementations are still trained as short-horizon autoregressive mappers without explicit mechanism-level constraints [3, 4, 13, 23]. Third, post hoc robustness and counterfactual studies provide useful diagnostics for intervention analysis, but their explanatory outputs are frequently external to the core training objective [17, 21, 24, 25].
An additional line of work particularly relevant to bridge integration shows that geometric enhancements can explicitly upweight structurally critical bridge edges in traffic graphs, improving information flow across topological bottlenecks and stabilizing long-horizon forecasting [8]. This complements studies that incorporate physical and infrastructure-linked characteristics, such as lane-level capacity cues and road function, suggesting that structureaware modeling is more reliable than purely correlation-driven fitting when intervention behavior matters [11, 20].
1

STGNN and dynamic-graph message passing models Strong local-to-regional spatial learning and competitive benchmark performance [1, 11, 19].
Sensitivity to graph specification, topological bottlenecks, and longrange dependency capture [7–9].
Retain graph inductive bias but strengthen long-range and structural reasoning.
Transformer and hybrid attention models Global receptive fields, crossscale fusion, and improved sequence modeling flexibility [3, 4, 14, 18].
Frequently used as autoregressive predictors with limited structuregrounded contextual reasoning, constraining interpretability and crosscity transfer [12, 13, 21].
Learn structure-aware latent states and constrain rollouts for interpretable, transferable dynamics.
Post hoc counterfactual and robustness analyses Better model diagnostics, stress testing, and explanatory insight [21, 24, 25].
Explanations are frequently external to training and may not enforce mechanistic consistency [17, 26].
Embed intervention sensitivity directly in training, not only in post hoc analysis.
Beyond empirical comparison, these streams also establish a clear theoretical basis for this concept. A first premise is systems-theoretic: traffic is a networked, non-Euclidean dynamic process in which topology, demand shifts, and disruptions jointly shape state evolution, so purely token-wise autoregression is insufficient for planning-oriented reasoning [15, 19, 22]. A second premise is representational: prior work on STGNN locality limits and transformerstyle global context suggests that robust forecasting under intervention requires both local propagation sensitivity and long-range relational modeling [3, 11, 14, 18]. A third premise is operational: evidence from traffic operations and digital-twin literature supports embedding physical plausibility expectations during training, rather than relying only on post hoc diagnostics, when models are intended for intervention support [5, 10, 24]. Together, these premises justify the integration of structure-aware conditioning, rollout training, and soft physical regularization used in SISTT.
Across these streams, a common gap remains: high benchmark fit does not automatically imply faithful modeling of congestion propagation dynamics under intervention and distribution shift. This motivates integrating structural context, latent-state reasoning, and rollout-consistent training in a single framework rather than treating explan

ns: high benchmark fit does not automatically imply faithful modeling of congestion propagation dynamics under intervention and distribution shift. This motivates integrating structural context, latent-state reasoning, and rollout-consistent training in a single framework rather than treating explanation and intervention sensitivity as post hoc add-ons.
3 Problem Statement Despite rapid progress, most contemporary spatio-temporal predictors are still trained as correlation-driven sequence regressors and therefore do not directly encode intervention-sensitive traffic state transitions [11, 19, 23]. In many transformer-oriented pipelines, forecasting is operationalized primarily as next-step or short-horizon autoregressive mapping over historical windows.
While this improves benchmark accuracy, it does not necessarily recover the underlying structural mechanisms that govern queue accumulation, spillback, and network recovery across heterogeneous road classes and capacities [3, 4, 23]. As a result, model behavior can be accurate yet weakly interpretable: it predicts what may happen, but provides limited evidence of why propagation patterns emerge [7, 21]. In parallel, unconstrained optimization can yield physically implausible trajectories under high-demand or disrupted regimes, particularly when model rollouts extend beyond one-step prediction [11, 22].
This limitation is most consequential when forecasts are used for intervention design rather than routine monitoring. Urban planning and operations require confidence that predicted responses to capacity changes or disruptions remain directionally and dynamically consistent, yet many high-performing models are not explicitly optimized for this objective [17, 24, 25]. The same representational weakness also affects transferability: models tuned to one city’s autoregressive regularities may degrade when deployed in networks with different topology, demand rhythms, and control policies [7, 12]. The core problem, therefore, is not only forecast error under normal conditions, but the absence of an explicit modeling framework for learning interpretable, structure-grounded dynamics that can generalize across intervention and city contexts.
2

For clarity, the paper in concept targets three main problems: autoregressive forecasting without deep structural understanding, limited interpretability of propagation mechanisms, and weak transferability across city and intervention contexts.
Contributions This concept paper makes the following contributions: • A structure-aware and intervention-sensitive spatio-temporal transformer formulation that integrates structural road context, latent state modeling, and rollout-consistent training for intervention-sensitive forecasting.
• A soft physical regularization framework that combines capacity plausibility and temporal smoothness constraints without hard-coded physical solvers.
• An intervention-oriented evaluation protocol that measures propagation fidelity and directional consistency under simulated disruptions, beyond conventional point-error metrics.
• A fairness-oriented empirical plan using harmonized baselines and component-level ablations to isolate which design choices drive robustness, stability, and transfer performance.
4 Proposed New Solution This concept paper proposes a structure-aware, intervention-sensitive spatio-temporal traffic transformer (SISTT) designed to model urban traffic as a constrained dynamic system rather than as an unconstrained sequence regression task. Conceptually, SISTT introduces latent traffic states that represent congestion buildup and dissipation over time, and it conditions attention on explicit structural context (e.g., topology role, lane-related capacity, and road hierarchy) so that predictions are grounded in network function rather than temporal pattern repetition alone [3, 11, 12]. In implementation terms, this structural context is injected through node-level capacity and road-class embeddings, together with structure-aware attention bias terms derived from adjacency and hierarchy relations.
Soft physical regularization discourages capacity-inconsistent transitions, while multi-step rollout training aligns optimization with stable sequential forecasting under perturbation [2, 13]. We use ”structure-aware and interventionsensitive” to denote modeling that conditions forecasts on explicit road structure and intervention perturbations, without claiming formal effect identification.
The conceptual framing treats traffic forecasting as intervention-sensitive dynamic system identification. Under this framing, temporal demand and network structure jointly govern state evolution, structural attributes provide explanatory context for response heterogeneity, and long-horizon stability becomes an explicit modeling target rather than an indirect byproduct of one-step fitting.
The central claim is that richer structure-aware context learning can improve interpretability and transferability, because the model is encouraged to learn structure-grounded traffic dynamics that remain meaningful across cities instead of memorizing dataset-specific autoregressive regularities [12, 21, 25]. We do not claim formal effe

ture-aware context learning can improve interpretability and transferability, because the model is encouraged to learn structure-grounded traffic dynamics that remain meaningful across cities instead of memorizing dataset-specific autoregressive regularities [12, 21, 25]. We do not claim formal effect identification from observational data; instead, we evaluate whether structural conditioning improves intervention-consistent predictive behavior.
The concept is guided by three linked research questions: 1. RQ1: To what extent does integrating explicit structural conditioning (e.g., hierarchy, capacity) mitigate performance degradation during non-recurring traffic interventions?
2. RQ2: How effectively does soft physical regularization constrain the generation of dynamically implausible trajectories under simulated demand and capacity perturbations?
3. RQ3: What is the measurable impact of multi-step rollout training on cross-network transferability and longhorizon stability compared to standard autoregressive optimization?
Variables and Scope The unit of analysis is link-level traffic states at 5-minute intervals on sensor-instrumented road networks. Independent variables are model design choices (structure conditioning, rollout training, and regularization strength), intervention design (type, location, and intensity), and evaluation horizon. Dependent variables are MAE/RMSE/MAPE, intervention reliability metrics (PE(a), DC(a)), and long-horizon stability indicators. Key controls include matched parameter budgets, common train/validation/test splits, fixed input window lengths, and repeated random seeds. Scope is limited to planning-level decision support on freeway-dominant benchmarks; results are not intended as direct claims about signal-phase optimization or fully urban intersection control.
3

Stage 1: Contextual Fusion & Encoding Stage 2: Multi-Step Rollout (Forecasting) Stage 3: Joint Optimization & Regularization Network Topology (Graph G) Structural Metadata (Lane Cap Ce, Hierarchy) Historical Traffic Window Transformer-Based State Encoder Structure-Aware Attention Bias Mutual Context Updates Latent Traffic State (Ht) Multi-Step Decoder (Transformer-based) Phase 1: One-Step Pretraining ( ˆYt+1) warm-up Predicted Trajectories ( ˆYt+1 to ˆYt+H) Unified Training Loss L = Lpred + λcapLcap + λconsLcons (accuracy + soft physical regularization + temporal smoothness) Ground Truth (Yt+1:t+H) Stage 4: Backpropagation Figure 1: Architecture of the Structure-Aware, Intervention-Sensitive Spatio-Temporal Traffic Transformer (SISTT), emphasizing the bidirectional contextual fusion in Stage 1. Gray nodes and arrows denote existing or baseline pipeline components, colored nodes and arrows denote proposed SISTT additions, and dashed arrows denote auxiliary training flow (warm-up/backpropagation).
5 Tentative Methodologies 5.1 Dataset and Graph Construction The empirical study will use two widely adopted benchmarks for comparability: PeMSD8 (PeMS-08) and METR-LA [19, 23, 26]. At sensor node i and time t, the input signal is represented as xi,t = [vi,t, qi,t, oi,t, mi,t], (1) where vi,t is speed (always used when available), qi,t is flow, oi,t is occupancy or density proxy, and mi,t is a channelavailability mask to keep PeMSD8 and METR-LA comparable when a variable is missing in one dataset. This makes speed a first-class target rather than a secondary diagnostic variable.
The road graph will be augmented with structural metadata, such as directional topology, hierarchy signals, and lane-related capacity proxies (including physical lane-count metadata where available), and all preprocessing assumptions will be reported explicitly to preserve reproducibility and facilitate fair cross-model interpretation [9, 12, 20]. Because capacity metadata in PeMS-style benchmarks can be incomplete or noisy, robustness checks will include 4

alternative capacity encodings: coarse tiers (low/medium/high) and percentile-based pseudo-capacity estimated from each link’s historical flow distribution. To directly examine transferability, evaluation splits will include both withindataset and cross-dataset transfer settings whenever sensor-feature compatibility permits [7, 12]. We also acknowledge a dataset realism limitation: these benchmarks are heavily highway-oriented, so claims about dense urban intersection behavior will be framed cautiously; where metadata permits, analyses will report a focused subset of links that better proxy urban arterial conditions.
5.2 Baseline Justification and Experimental Controls Comparative evaluation will include representative local message-passing STGNNs, dynamic-graph variants, and transformer or hybrid baselines so that gains can be interpreted relative to established methodological families rather than a single comparator [1, 3, 4, 11, 19]. Baseline selection is purpose-driven: STGNNs test strong local spatial inductive bias, dynamic-graph models test adaptation to topology-sensitive shifts, and transformer/hybrid models test long-range context capture under common autoregressive training. Baselines will explicitly include autoregressive transformer implementations and structure-aware variants to isolate the effect of contextual structural conditioning on robustness and generalization. To address fairness concerns, experiments will include a controlled component ladder under matched model width/depth and training budget: vanilla autoregressive transformer, rollout-only tuning, structure-conditioning-only, regularization-only, and full SISTT. Training budgets, data splits, and reporting conventions will be harmonized where possible to reduce protocol-driven variance in conclusions and improve evidential clarity [6, 16].
5.3 Optimization Strategy for Dynamic Consistency Optimization will proceed in two linked phases, beginning with one-step supervised pretraining for stable initialization and followed by multi-step rollout tuning with dynamic-consistency regularization via temporal smoothness constraints. This sequencing is intended to preserve short-horizon accuracy while explicitly shaping long-horizon behavior, consistent with recent evidence that multi-scale and adaptive temporal learning improves deployment-oriented robustness [2, 13].
5.4 Conceptual Formulation The proposed methodology is organized as a dynamic reasoning pipeline rather than a pure next-step autoregressive predictor. First, the model receives three complementary inputs: (i) a historical traffic window, (ii) road-network topology, and (iii) structural road attributes such as lane-related capacity proxies. For each node-time token, dynamic, temporal, and structural information is combined as • Traffic transformer: A transformer encoder-decoder operating on graph-indexed traffic tokens with topologyaware attention bias.
• Structure-aware and intervention-sensitive: Predictions are c

d capacity proxies. For each node-time token, dynamic, temporal, and structural information is combined as • Traffic transformer: A transformer encoder-decoder operating on graph-indexed traffic tokens with topologyaware attention bias.
• Structure-aware and intervention-sensitive: Predictions are conditioned on structural drivers (topology, hierarchy, and capacity proxies) that mediate congestion propagation.
• Soft physical regularization: Physical plausibility is enforced through penalties in the loss rather than hard constraints.
• Dynamic consistency: Multi-step rollouts are stabilized through temporal smoothness constraints that penalize unrealistically large step-to-step speed changes.
zi,t = Wxxi,t + Etod s(t) + Edow d(t) + Ehier hi + WC ˜Ci + Ecls ci , (2) where s(t) and d(t) index time-of-day and day-of-week, hi is hierarchy level, ˜Ci is normalized capacity proxy, and ci is road functional class. Structure-aware attention is further guided by a bias term Bij = β1Aij + β2 exp  −dG(i, j) τd  + β31[hi = hj], (3) so links that are adjacent, topologically near, or functionally similar can be preferentially attended.
The latent traffic state is defined explicitly as Ht = Encoder(Zt−L+1:t, G, B).
(4) 5

Conceptually, this state is computed by the encoder from the recent look-back window together with the road graph and structure-aware attention context. In practical terms, this state is a collection of node-level representations (one per monitored link) that summarize current speed regime, queue pressure, and likely spillback pathways. As shown in Figure 1, this design is decomposed into contextual fusion, latent-state formation, rollout forecasting, and regularized optimization so the architectural flow is explicit before the training objectives are introduced. We do not assume direct semantic identifiability of individual latent dimensions; instead, interpretability is evaluated through aggregate alignment with known congestion regimes and structural bottlenecks.
Figure 1 summarizes this four-stage architecture and shows how structural context, rollout forecasting, and training regularization interact.
Second, forecasting is performed as a controlled rollout from the latent state across horizon H, so the model is trained to produce coherent trajectories instead of isolated one-step outputs. This choice is important for planningoriented use cases because decision support depends on how congestion evolves over multiple steps, not only on the immediate next timestamp.
The rollout framing in Figure 1 also aligns with the intervention focus of this concept paper, where local disruptions can trigger delayed and spatially distributed downstream effects that should be explainable in terms of network structure.
Third, training balances three objectives: predictive accuracy, capacity plausibility, and dynamic consistency.
Predictive accuracy targets closeness to observed traffic variables. Capacity plausibility discourages patterns that exceed realistic roadway limits for sustained periods. Dynamic consistency is implemented as a temporal smoothness (gradient-penalty) constraint so multi-step rollouts do not produce physically implausible jumps between consecutive 5-minute predictions. The loss block in Figure 1 summarizes this balance, which is also written as L = Lpred + λcapLcap + λconsLcons, (5) where λcap and λcons control the strength of physical and dynamic regularization relative to forecast fit. A clearer way to view Lcap is in two steps. First, for each edge e and prediction step t, compute excess flow as xe,t = max(0, ˆqe,t−Ce), where Ce is known or proxied capacity. Second, aggregate these exceedances as an average squared penalty, Lcap = Avge,t  x2 e,t  .
(6) This means there is no penalty when predicted flow is below capacity, while larger violations are penalized more strongly. The capacity penalty is applied only during rollout training and not during one-step pretraining. Because speed is central to both PeMS datasets and operational decisions, the predictive term is made explicit as Lpred = αv MAE(ˆv, v) + αq MAE(ˆq, q) + αo MAE(ˆo, o), (7) with αv > αq, αo and masked channels removed when unavailable. The dynamic term is defined as

not during one-step pretraining. Because speed is central to both PeMS datasets and operational decisions, the predictive term is made explicit as Lpred = αv MAE(ˆv, v) + αq MAE(ˆq, q) + αo MAE(ˆo, o), (7) with αv > αq, αo and masked channels removed when unavailable. The dynamic term is defined as a temporal smoothness penalty on predicted speed gradients: Lcons = Avgi,t h max (0, |ˆvi,t+1 −ˆvi,t| −∆max)2i , (8) where ∆max is the maximum physically plausible speed change for one 5-minute interval. This penalty only activates when the model predicts acceleration or deceleration spikes beyond that threshold. Together with the two-stage training schedule, this simple constraint is key to keeping Stage 2 rollout forecasting stable over long horizons for intervention planning.
5.5 Evaluation, Intervention Testing, and Interpretation Evaluation will combine conventional error metrics (MAE, RMSE, MAPE) with intervention-oriented stress tests involving synthetic capacity reductions, demand surges, and localized network disruptions [17, 19, 24, 26]. Because PeMSD8 and METR-LA are sampled at 5-minute intervals, results will be reported across both horizon bands and demand periods. Horizon bands are short (τ = 1–3, 5–15 min), medium (τ = 4–12, 20–60 min), and long (τ = 13–24, 65–120 min). Demand periods are overnight (00:00–05:59), AM peak (06:00–09:59), midday (10:00–15:59), PM peak (16:00–19:59), and evening (20:00–23:59), with separate reporting for weekdays and weekends when sample counts allow.
To improve real-world applicability, evaluation will also report operationally interpretable outputs: expected delay increase by corridor class, predicted recovery-time window after disruption, and confidence bands for intervention effects.
These outputs are intended to support planner-facing decision tables (ranked alternatives) rather than single-score model selection.
6

Beyond point error, the analysis will track congestion propagation realism, recovery dynamics, cumulative network impact, and directional consistency of counterfactual responses under matched interventions [21, 25]. The protocol will also include transferability checks across datasets or city domains, alongside interpretability analyses of latent trajectories and attention patterns to test whether learned explanations align with known structural bottlenecks and flow pathways [3, 4, 11, 12]. Planned latent-state diagnostics include correlation tests against known bottleneck links, clustering latent states against congestion phases (free-flow, buildup, spillback, recovery), and visualization of state evolution during disruption windows.
For an intervention scenario a (e.g., targeted capacity reduction), intervention effect is evaluated relative to a non-intervention baseline as ∆(a) t+τ = ˆY(a) t+τ −ˆY(0) t+τ.
To make propagation fidelity explicit, we define a speedfocused propagation error using graph-distance weights wi = exp(−di/τd), where di is shortest-path distance from the intervention locus: PE(a) = 1 H H X τ=1 P i wi    ∆(a,v) i,t+τ −∆∗(a,v) i,t+τ     P i wi .
(9) Here, ∆(a,v) is predicted speed change under intervention and ∆∗(a,v) is observed speed change. Lower PE(a) indicates better matching of both propagation magnitude and spatial spread. We also report directional consistency, DC(a) = 1 NH X i,τ 1 h sign(∆(a,v) i,t+τ) = sign(∆∗(a,v) i,t+τ ) i , (10) so intervention reliability is assessed jointly by point accuracy, propagation error, and sign-consistent response over time.
6 Expected Scholarly Contribution This work is expected to contribute a conceptually grounded and empirically testable pathway for aligning highperforming traffic forecasting with intervention-capable decision support. The contribution is not framed as replacing existing paradigms, but as integrating their strongest ideas: graph-structured inductive bias, global attention-based context modeling, and counterfactual sensitivity, while adding explicit dynamic-consistency constraints within training. A limitation of this study is reliance on highway-oriented benchmarks; extension to signalized urban intersections remains future work. A second limitation is deployment complexity: city-scale inference and strict latency budgets may require sparse attention variants and infrastructure-aware model compression.
If validated, the proposed framework can strengthen the practical utility of urban traffic prediction for planning and policy by reducing the gap between benchmark accuracy and realistic intervention response behavior [12, 21, 25].
In practical terms, the intended policy pathway is scenario ranking: planners compare candidate interventions by predicted congestion spread, recovery time, and reliability metrics before implementation. More broadly, this advances the thesis that better modeling of underlying road-network structure is a practical route toward more interp

pathway is scenario ranking: planners compare candidate interventions by predicted congestion spread, recovery time, and reliability metrics before implementation. More broadly, this advances the thesis that better modeling of underlying road-network structure is a practical route toward more interpretable and generalizable traffic intelligence, particularly under cross-city transfer and non-recurring operational conditions.
